data.title: Realty Income: Attractive For Income Investors, But Mr. Market May Be Trying To Tell Us Something (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Realty Income (O) has been a popular REIT amongst investors for years, especially those seeking income.

With a long track record of paying dividends and likely to continue for the foreseeable future, O deserves their spot in any income-focused investor's portfolio.

Realty Income's A-rated balance sheet, size & scale advantages, and long operating history are something to be commended. However, with the recent underperformance over the past few years, Mr. Market may be trying to tell us something.

One has to wonder if the REIT could continue to underperform some peers going forward as a result of its portfolio, size, and investments that haven't moved the needle as expected.

In this article, I discuss Realty Income's latest quarter, fundamentals, and why their underperformance may be an indication of something investors should be cautious of going forward.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Realty Income in May in an article titled: Why Realty Income Deserves A Spot In Every Dividend Investor's Portfolio. During their Q1, the REIT saw modest AFFO growth of a penny to $1.06 from the previous quarter.

And despite the decline in occupancy, revenue managed to increase due to annual rent escalators. Year-over-year, their bottom line increased 2.9%.

At a price of $55.78, O offered upside to their price target of $61.38. Since, O is up 5% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up nearly double at 9.84%.

Latest Quarter Saw A Modest AFFO Decline

Realty Income reported their Q2 earnings at the beginning of the month and saw AFFO decline modestly from the previous quarter and year. AFFO amounted to $1.05, down a penny from Q1 and the previous year's quarter.

Core FFO, however, was in-line with analysts' estimates at $1.05, while revenue of $1.41 billion beat by a solid $0.8 million. This increased from $1.34 billion the year prior, likely due to annual rent escalators.

The rent recapture rate was 103.4% across 346 leases, which represented $97 million in annual rent. But this was down from the previous year's second quarter that saw rent recapture at 105.7%. But the number of leases was significantly less at 99.

The REIT continued its focus in Europe, which they entered into in 2019. Management made 57% of its $1.2 billion in investments in Europe and the rest in the U.S.

And this is likely due to them being able to see higher spreads, which investments had a weighted-average cash yield of 7.2%. In the U.S., investment spreads were 7%.

Moreover, spreads also compressed from 2024 as investments had a weighted-average cash yield of 7.9% in Europe during that time. In the U.S., this was 7.6%, 60 basis points higher.

O also disposed of 73 properties in the quarter, most of these vacant for proceeds of $117 million. Occupancy also increased 10 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 98.6%. But on an annualized basis, occupancy declined slightly from 98.8%.

Guidance Raise

Despite the modest decline in AFFO and occupancy, management was still able to raise AFFO and investment guidance during the quarter. Previously, O was targeting $4 billion in investments and AFFO in a range of $4.22 to $4.28.

Now, management expects $5 billion in investments for the year and AFFO in a range of $4.24 - $4.28, representing a growth rate of 1.67% at midpoint.

Management stated in Q4 that guidance assumed a $0.04 impact of disposing of underperforming tenants. This was also significantly lower than 2024, which saw AFFO growth of 4.8%.

Disappointing growth could also be another reason for their underperformance, but this does represent the 15th consecutive year of growth for the REIT.

What Mr. Market May Also Be Pricing In

One of the reasons investors may love Realty Income, besides their long history of paying monthly dividends, is their size, which gives them advantages over peers.

Being one of the few A-rated REITs, this gives them cost advantages, as higher-rated REITs typically see higher spreads over their cost of capital.

As a result, this leads to accretion, which leads to higher growth, thus often positively impacting the share price. Overall, REITs are usually lower growth stocks.

The high-quality, retail REITs often see 3%–6% AFFO annual growth. For comparison, Agree Realty (ADC), who recently raised their guidance as well, now expects growth of 4% for the year.

VICI Properties (VICI), another one of my favorite REITs, is expecting slightly higher AFFO growth of 4.4% for 2025.

Here's Realty Income's investor deck. And you can see when calculating their WACC, they use a retained free cash flow percentage of zero.

Below is the comparison to Agree Realty's investor deck. You can see how management mentions that adding a 0% cost to retained free cash flow improves your cost of capital artificially by roughly 80 basis points.

In other words, this lowers your cost of capital to make spreads appear to be more accretive. While I don't know the correct cost to assign, it seems that a 0% cost distorts spreads and creates the appearance that they are higher than they actually are.

Realty Income's management stated during Q4 '24 earnings that they managed to achieve investment spreads of 243 basis points. Here's what their CEO stated:

Throughout the year, we remained disciplined in our capital allocation strategy, investing $3.9 billion at a 7.4% weighted-average initial cash yield. We funded these investments with attractively-priced capital, resulting in a 243 basis point investment spread, exceeding our historical average of 150 basis points.

You can see from Agree Realty's investor deck that achieving spreads of over 150 bps should result in high accretion, which would benefit shareholders. Moreover, this level of accretion should translate to respectable bottom line growth.

However, Realty Income is only expecting 1.67% growth this year. And while this could have something to do with their portfolio mostly consisting of non-investment grade tenants' underperforming in recent years, possibly achieving lower spreads than appeared is something investors should be cautious of going forward.

While most likely invest for the monthly dividend, which the REIT raised 0.2% in June to $0.269, investors want the potential to see capital appreciation as well.

Moreover, in the future, this may impact their ability to sufficiently cover the monthly dividend. For now, it remains safe with a projected AFFO payout ratio of 75.3%.

Although REITs have lagged due to the high-interest rate environment, ADC is actually in the green in comparison, while O is down over 3% in the past five years.

While the S&P (SP500) has continued to have a strong performance over a 5-year period, the sector has lagged, partly due to the macro environment. But REITs should be viewed primarily as income vehicles, not growth machines.

And looking at total returns over a longer period, you can see Agree Realty has done well against Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO) in total returns, up 280% compared to nearly 286% for the ETF. O trails behind both significantly, up approximately 106%.

Attractive Valuation

Despite this, O remains attractive, especially if you're a retiree looking for stable income.

So, they have their place inside an income-focused portfolio despite their underperformance. Moreover, they could see tailwinds from expected lower base rates with potential capital appreciation.

Currently, Realty Income's forward P/AFFO multiple sits at 13.77x. For comparison purposes, this is lower than the following peers' average of 14.14x using each REIT's current forward multiple.

Agree Realty: 17.09x

Simon Property Group (SPG): 13.91x

NNN REIT (NNN): 12.08x

Federal Realty Trust (FRT): 13.49x

Wall Street currently rates O a hold with a price target of $62.39, roughly 6% upside.

They do offer a more attractive upside if they can return to their normal multiple of 17.21x. But this will be highly dependent on where interest rates are and if they can achieve accretive spreads and manage to offload non-performing tenants.

Furthermore, I would like to see further dispositions of non-rated tenants to strengthen their portfolio. Management also initiated a share repurchase program that I would like to see them take advantage of before considering upgrading the REIT to a buy again.

Risks & Bottom Line

Realty Income's biggest risk is underperformance going forward. Their large size does give them some advantages, as each tenant only makes up roughly 1% of rent.

But their exposure to troubled tenants like Red Lobster, Walgreens (WBA), Rite-Aid, Dollar Tree (DLTR), Zips Car Wash (who previously filed for bankruptcy back in February), and AtHome, who filed for bankruptcy this past June.

Management stated they expect resolutions going forward and were able to achieve a 94% rent recapture rate during the quarter. But a further weakening economy could result in additional bankruptcies, which could result in rent loss.

Realty Income remains attractive from a valuation standpoint when compared to peers, but their underperformance is something that could continue going forward.

While they could see tailwinds from lower interest rates and subsequent upside, I agree with Wall Street's rating and am downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

data.title: Strategy: The S&P 500 Potential Inclusion And Arbitrage Play
data.description:
Strategy Inc. (NASDAQ:MSTR), previously known as MicroStrategy Incorporated, released its second-quarter results a few weeks ago, reporting revenues of $114.5 million, an increase of 3% on a year-over-year basis, but more importantly, a staggering GAAP net income of $10 billion. In this article, I will not delve into the company's earnings report because it would be a distraction from the main thesis I'm going to present, which is the possibility of Strategy finally joining the S&P 500 Index, perhaps in the next few weeks. So why did I mention the net income of $10 billion? Because this number may be Strategy's last piece of the puzzle before it can join the world's most famous equity index.

Don't get me wrong. This article is not about the fundamentals of Strategy as a company. To be honest, I've been closely studying this company for more than a year, conducting comprehensive research on its unique aspects, and hoping to share it with you soon. The piece I'm sharing today is more for the traders in the crowd, which I can't neglect, especially when I see a near-term opportunity that might be boiling under the surface.

Here, we will focus on a special situation that might be evolving around Strategy, as the company seems like a solid candidate to join the S&P 500 Index. Such inclusion may have significant ramifications for its shareholders starting the announcement date until the official inclusion date, and perhaps more importantly, for arbitrageurs looking to exploit the situation.

The Setting: Clearing the Hurdles

It's not an easy task to join such a well-respected index like the S&P 500, the "elite club" of America's finest enterprises. Basically, every company that may be considered as a nominee for inclusion must first check a few criteria. In this chapter, we will examine each one of the criteria and see if Strategy fits in.

The first criterion is to be a U.S.-based and U.S.-listed company. More technically, the company must be domiciled in the U.S. and listed on a U.S. exchange. Of course, Strategy checks this box easily, as it's incorporated in Delaware, headquartered in Tysons Corner, Virginia, and has a primary listing on the Nasdaq.

The second criterion is a minimum market cap threshold of $22.7 billion (as of July 2025). Strategy checks this box easily, too, with its market cap of $97 billion. The third criterion is the public float. At least 50% of Strategy's shares must be in public hands. The float is 93% of the total outstanding shares, which is more than enough. For those who are curious, it's not 100% because its Executive Chairman, Michael Saylor, holds the vast majority of the other 7% in Class B shares (which, by the way, gives him more than 42% voting power in the company).

The fourth criterion is around liquidity, with two sub-conditions. First, the "Float-Adjusted Liquidity Ratio" (known in short as FALR) must be equal to or higher than 0.75x at the time of addition. The FALR is defined by dividing the annual dollar value traded by the float-adjusted market cap. Over the last twelve months, more than $1.4 trillion (with a "T") worth of Strategy's shares have been traded, and the company's float-adjusted market cap is approximately $91 billion ($97 x 93%). This gives us an FALR ratio of more than 15x. In addition, at least 250,000 shares in each of the six months prior to the evaluation date must have been traded. In Strategy's case, more than 300 million shares have been traded, on average, in each of the last six months.

The fifth criterion is the organizational structure of the company. To join the S&P 500, the company must be a corporation with a common stock (i.e., it can't be a closed-end fund, ETF, SPAC, limited partnership, etc.). Strategy is indeed a corporation with a common stock.

The sixth criterion is about regulatory filing. In simple terms, the company must file reports with the SEC. Strategy does that. Still all good.

Many of the readers would probably start to ask themselves what has prevented Strategy from joining the S&P 500 so far if it checks so many boxes. After all. Does the most famous equity index in the world discriminate against companies with orange logos or with eccentric CEOs? Well, the answer (I hope!) is no and no. And now we are starting to get to the really interesting part: The seventh criterion is about the financial viability of the company. This was one, if not the major, obstacle for Strategy from joining the S&P 500. Put simply, there are two financial sub-conditions here: (1) the sum of the company's GAAP net earnings over the most recent four consecutive quarters must be positive, and (2) the most recent quarter's GAAP net earnings must also be positive. This must have sounded like an easy task for a company that started to buy Bitcoin back in the summer of 2020 and generated an unrealized return of approximately $25 billion, or 55%, on its Bitcoin holdings. But this is not the case, folks, and like everything in life, it all goes back to the tiny details, and in the case of Strategy: accounting quirks.

Although the company started to buy Bitcoin at an average price of $10-50K in 2020, for almost five years, accounting principles worked against it in every single way. Why? Because until December 2023, the Financial Accounting Standards Board (known as the "FASB"), which is the accounting authority that establishes and updates accounting and financial reporting standards in the U.S., instructed companies like Strategy, which holds digital assets like Bitcoin, to measure its holding at cost, net of any impairment losses incurred since acquisition. Without getting too deep and boring, impairment losses were recognized as expenses, and gains were not recorded until realized upon sale. Put simply, if you held a volatile asset like Bitcoin, you recorded impairments when Bitcoin fell and could never reverse them when Bitcoin went up, not to mention any gains, unless you sold the Bitcoins. And if you were HODL-ing, like Strategy declares it adheres to, you had a huge gap between the carrying value of your Bitcoins on the balance sheet and the Bitcoins' true market value. Just to give you some color, by the end of 2024, the carrying value of Strategy's Bitcoins was $24 billion, while the market value was $42 billion, an enormous discount of more than 40%!

You can already understand that the old accounting principles created a twisted P&L and balance sheet for Strategy and prevented it from recording any net profits from its Bitcoin holdings' significant appreciation. The inevitable result was a technical blocker from getting into the S&P 500 index.

However, this saga came to a close in December 2023, when the FASB issued an update that finally allowed Strategy to measure its Bitcoin holdings at fair value, with gains and losses recognized in the net income in each reporting period. Strategy adopted this guidance on January 1, 2025, and as you can imagine, with the Bitcoin price soaring above $100K, the company's P&L in Q2-2025 delivered a staggering net profit of $10 billion, most of it because of the $14 billion gain from the holdings revaluation. Now, this number is a major tailwind for joining the S&P 500 because it achieves two key objectives: first, it makes the company profitable on a LTM (last 12 months) basis ($4.8 billion), and second, it achieves profitability on a "most recent quarter" basis ($10 billion).

Moving on to the last two criteria, which are probably the most debatable ones. Why? Because they are not technical but subjective. The eighth criterion is about sector representation. Basically, the index committee that will discuss Strategy's prospects to join the S&P 500 will consider whether this company contributes to the sector balance of the index. In the case of Strategy, it would be fair to assume it's going to be classified under the information technology sector, which accounts for almost 35% of the index. However, and this leads us to the final criterion, "the joker": the committee may use its discretion to decide whether Strategy is indeed an IT company, and more importantly, whether it gives the green light in general for the "the world's first and largest Bitcoin treasury company" to join America's grandest index of them all. Put simply, even though the company is checking most, if not all, the boxes, the committee can exercise its discretion and subjective judgment to make the final decision, regardless of all the other criteria.

So what will the committee decide to do? Here start the speculations. The base assumption that will also lead us to the second part of this article is that the committee will add Strategy to the S&P 500 index. Yes, Strategy's business model is the first of its kind in corporate America and may be hard to digest by traditional institutions such as S&P Global, the organization that is responsible for creating, maintaining, and governing the S&P 500 index. However, I believe it's more likely than not that Strategy will be allowed to join the index, given its size, financial viability, liquidity, and corporate structure. Moreover, in the current environment, I find it hard to believe that the committee will take any controversial decisions, especially when the White House is showing so much support for the digital asset.

So if we assume it's not a matter of "if" but a matter of "when," then let's discuss a little bit about the "when".

The index committee, part of S&P Global (SPGI), meets on a monthly basis. Since Strategy released its latest earnings on July 31, the most likely window for an announcement falls in August or September. Any lack of announcement after September may require a reevaluation of the thesis. However, if such an announcement does happen, it would trigger a rebalancing window in which some of the world's biggest ETFs and mutual funds must make room for Strategy - and load up on its shares - setting the stage for the arbitrage play we are about to explore right next.

The Play: Riding the Dollar Inflow Wave

Now, for the most interesting part - the implications such index inclusion can have on Strategy's stock.

You see, this company is not a casual candidate for the S&P 500 index. The average market cap of a constituent in the index is approximately $38 billion. Moreover, 28% of the companies in the index have a market cap that is below the $22.7 billion threshold (nevertheless, this is not a "violation" leading to removal on its own). Strategy, if included in the index, would be ranked in the 71st percentile of the index.

And what would be the weight assigned to Strategy? The S&P 500 is a float-adjusted market-cap weighted index. In other words, it's using a factor called the Investable Weight Factor ("IWF"), which is multiplied by the market cap of each company, and this factor basically removes shares that are not available to the public. We mentioned it before: by removing Strategy's Class B shares, which account for 7% of the company's total shares outstanding, we are getting an IWF of 93%. Multiplying Strategy's market cap of $97 billion by 93% gives us a float-adjusted market cap of $91 billion. Dividing this number by the index total float-adjusted market cap of ~$54.3 trillion, the weight of Strategy turns out to be 0.17%.

You may think that 0.17% is a small number. Think again. Imagine that if Strategy is accepted to join the S&P 500 index, many ETFs and mutual funds that emulate this index will have to buy shares of Strategy to rebalance their portfolio. This means that these funds will have to allocate 0.17% of their assets to buy shares of the company. Just to give you some color, I looked for the biggest funds tracking the S&P 500 and calculated how much dollar inflows may go to Strategy's stock (all calculations are based on market caps and assets as of August 21, 2025):

Fund Type Assets
SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) ETF $653 billion
iShares Core S&P 500 (IVV) ETF $640 billion
Vanguard S&P 500 (VOO) ETF $711 billion
SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 (SPLG) ETF $79 billion
Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight (RSP) (**) ETF $72 billion
Vanguard 500 Index Fund (VFIAX) MF $1,490 billion
Fidelity 500 Index Fund (FXAIX) MF $682 billion
Schwab S&P 500 Index Fund (SWPPX) MF $120 billion
State Street S&P 500 Index Fund (SVSPX) MF $2 billion
iShares S&P 500 Index Fund (BSPIX) MF $19 billion
T Rowe Price S&P 500 Index Fund MF $8 billion
Total Assets - $4,622 billion
Allocated to Strategy (in case of inclusion) 0.17% $7.7 billion
% of Strategy Float-Adjusted Market Cap - 8.5%

** The Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight (RSP) holds the same stocks but each at an equal weight of 0.20%.

As you can see, an inclusion of Strategy may lead funds to allocate at least $7.7 billion to buy the company's shares, or 8.5% of the float ("at least," because there are many more funds around the world that track the S&P 500). The end game, for a trader looking to speculate on such inclusion, may be a quick spike in the stock price of Strategy or even a post-announcement rally until the rebalance date.

A good example of such a post-announcement rally can be found in the stock of Tesla (TSLA), which surged 60% in 2020 from the day before its inclusion announcement to its actual inclusion date a month later. When Tesla joined the S&P 500, its float-adjusted market cap was $304 billion, making it the largest S&P 500 addition ever, but Strategy may not be that far below on the list of the largest companies joining the index.

A more recent and crypto-related example would be Coinbase (COIN), which joined the S&P 500 in May 2025 and saw its stock surge following the announcement.

Another interesting tailwind that may only add some fuel to any potential rally is Strategy's short interest. The latest data points to 22.3 million shares borrowed for short-selling. Using the company's latest closing price, that's equal to $7.7 billion, or 8.5% of Strategy's float-adjusted market cap.

Short Interest (Volume) 22.3 million
Short Interest (USD) $7.7 billion
% of Strategy Float-Adjusted Market Cap 8.5%
Days to Cover 1.6 days

Adding that to the (minimum) $7.7 billion of projected inflows, which also equals 8.5% of the float-adjusted market cap, and the result may be a significant upward pressure on the stock price when funds are loading the stock and short-sellers are quickly trying to cover. I'm not suggesting that a short squeeze is my base case because, as you can see, the "days-to-cover" are quite low (using the median daily USD volume from the last 12 months), but short-sellers being under pressure may be a nice kicker.

Risks to Consider

As the headline of the article suggests, this play is not a long-term one. It's a short-term arb play. It's not only a speculation whether the S&P 500 Index Committee will add Strategy to the index but also on the follow-up move of the stock price. For the inclusion part, you can ask the broken-heart arbitrageurs of advertising firm AppLovin (APP), who speculated on the company joining the index in June 2025 and found no love from the committee. For the stock price movement following an inclusion, there is always the possibility that such inclusion is already priced in the stock, making it a non-event, or that a sudden Bitcoin decline would eclipse any inclusion/exclusion decision. And for a company like Strategy, which trades at an mNAV of more than 2.0 for most part of the last 24 months and has an implied volatility of 57%, embedded premium and volatility are not bugs; they are apparently features. In other words, there is already so much hype around this company that the downside risk is high from the beginning.

On top of that, on September 17, 2025, the Fed is expected to announce its interest rate decision. As you can see in the chart below, market participants are giving almost an 80% probability for a rate cut, and you can assume that market positioning is structured according to such probability. Now, what will happen if the Fed decides not to cut? Things may get ugly really fast, making any inclusion of Strategy a completely irrelevant event for traders who will have much more important rebalancing work on their mind.

The Verdict

The next few weeks will be very interesting for Strategy. The company seems to check all the boxes for joining the S&P 500 index, and a positive action of the index committee in late August or in early September can trigger a new rally of the stock. Such an inclusion may bring a wave of at least $7.7 billion going into the stock (8.5% of the float-adjusted market cap), while short-sellers, "sitting" on the same market cap slice of 8.5%, get caught in the middle of it.

History provides us with a mixed picture of the prospects of this trade. While we've seen examples of stocks rallying hard because of such inclusion (Tesla in 2020), we've also witnessed heartbroken arbitrageurs lamenting unexpected rejections (AppLovin in 2025).

Traders should also be aware of the macro risks going into the committee decision, meaning the Fed interest rate decision on September 17. For now, it seems like everyone is on the "cut rates" side of the boat, so keep an eye on that angle too, because if Powell decides to hold rates, the market's response may be ugly.

While the committee will always retain its discretion and subjective judgment in excluding Strategy from the index, I believe it's more likely than not that it will eventually approve its inclusion. As I see it, the company's size, financial viability, liquidity, and corporate structure tilt the odds in its favor and support its inclusion in the S&P 500. Combining this with the expected inflows from index funds, I rate Strategy as a highly speculative "Buy".

Thanks for reading!

data.title: Nektar Therapeutics: Fairly Valued, High-Quality Biotech With Multiple Near-Term Catalysts
data.description:
Summary

Following the recent success of the REZOLVE-AD data readout, Nektar Therapeutics' (NASDAQ:NKTR) rezpegaldesleukin ("REZPEG") has become the best-in-class alternative mechanism of action (MoA) therapy for moderate-severe atopic dermatitis. We believe that REZPEG has a strong potential to become the default second-line therapy after IL4/13 drugs (like EBGLYSS from Eli Lilly (LLY) and DUPIXENT from Regeneron (REGN)).

Despite being priced in line with the price targets outlined in our risk-adjusted NPV (rNPV) model, Nektar stands to benefit from multiple potential upsides in the near term, which could make a good buying opportunity. Over the long term, REZPEG could be a strong asset, promising to offer dermatologists a much-needed treatment option for IL4/13 non-responders that do not carry JAK-inhibitor side effects. We assign a Hold rating on Nektar for now but will closely monitor the stock, looking for a potential buying opportunity through 2025 and 2026.

Introduction

Nektar Therapeutics has a single clinical-stage asset in active development, REZPEG (previously NKTR-355)-a PEGylated, recombinant interleukin-2 engineered to be Treg-selective. While native IL2 activates both T-effector and T-regulatory (Treg) cells, REZPEG preferentially activates T-regulatory cells to dampen the autoimmune response of atopic dermatitis. REZPEG is currently being investigated in two key trials: REZOLVE-AD for Atopic Dermatitis (AD) and REZOLVE-AA for Alopecia Areata (AA). Having just read out positive 16-week data (the main efficacy endpoint in AD trials), REZOLVE-AD is on course to provide long-term maintenance data at 52 weeks in Q1 2026; this potentially allows REZPEG to show further improvement in the already competitive efficacy profile it has already shown in the 16-week part of the trial. A Phase 3 trial in AD is planned to be initiated in H1 2026, following the success of REZOLVE-AD. Additionally, Nektar is expecting to read out data from REZOLVE-AA in December 2025.

Key Product Pipeline

Nektar has the following assets under active development:

1. NKTR-355 (rezpegaldesleukin), a treg cell stimulator in Phase 2 trials for AD and AA.

2. NKTR-255 is an investigational IL15 receptor agonist designed to expand NK and CD8+ T cells to enhance anti-tumor immunity in Phase 1/2 trials for B-cell lymphoma.

3. NKTR-0165 is a preclinical TNFR2 agonist antibody for inflammatory diseases currently in the IND-enabling stage.

With NKTR-355 being the most advanced candidate, the majority of anticipated catalysts are associated with the additional data stemming from REZOLVE-AD (REZPEG for AD) and REZOLVE-AA (REZPEG for AA) Phase 2 trials, as well as the smooth progression to Phase 3.

Atopic Dermatitis Efficacy Profile

From our detailed review of the Moderate-Severe Atopic Dermatitis clinical data, we can see that the sector is currently made up of a number of FDA-approved drugs, initially presenting as a highly competitive indication.

  • DUPIXENT (dupilimab), an IL4 receptor alpha blocker, is the market leader. Developed in collaboration between Regeneron (REGN) and Sanofi (SNY), DUPIXENT is a wildly successful blockbuster, accounting for the majority of sales in the segment as a first-line leader.
  • EBGLYSS (lebrikizumab) is an IL13 inhibitor showing best-in-class efficacy in the IL4/13 MoA. Recently launched by Eli Lilly, EBGLYSS is on track to be a blockbuster and a competitive threat to DUPIXENT.
  • Adbry (tralokinumab) is an IL13 inhibitor commercialized by LEO Pharma. As a "me-three" drug without best-in-class efficacy, Adbry seems to be on tenuous footing in the commercial space. However, Adbry seems to have had a surprisingly successful launch year.
  • NEMLUVIO (nemolizumab) blocks IL31 signaling, a key driver of itch (pruritus). Despite not directly targeting the sources of the inflammatory response (and hence a more muted efficacy profile), NEMLUVIO has also had a successful launch year as the first entrant with a differentiated mechanism of action.
  • RINVOQ, CIBINQO, and OPZELURA are JAK-inhibitors (JAKis) offering a potent immunosuppressive/immunomodulating effect and therefore lead in terms of efficacy. However, a boxed warning about death places JAKis as a last-resort therapy carrying a prohibitive safety profile for the majority of AD patients.

Considering this, it is clear that REZPEG is a very strong contender for a specific position in the market-a best-in-class second-line alternative therapy for IL4/13 non-responders. The IL4/13 MoA, despite being a leader in non-JAKi efficacy, has two caveats that reinforce the commercial opportunity for REZPEG.

Up to 50% of Patients Are Considered Non-responders

Typically, patients who are not able to achieve an improvement of 50% or greater in the Eczema Area and Severity Index (EASI-50) score are considered to be non-responders. DUPIXENT, the market leader, has around 35% of patients unable to secure an EASI-50 response. Adbry has more than 50% of patients as non-responders. Whereas, EBGLYSS did not report EASI-50 for their Phase 3 trials, ADvocate 1 and ADvocate 2.

DUPIXENT is Associated With Elevated Levels of Conjunctivitis

DUPIXENT is known to cause conjunctivitis, a listed side effect included in the approved treatment label, and a common reason for patients to discontinue the drug. In SOLO 1, 5% of the patients reported conjunctivitis. In the longer-term CHRONOS study (in combination with topical corticosteroids), 12% of patients reported conjunctivitis over a 52-week duration. With topical corticosteroids (TCS) being taken by 78.5% of patients, the CHRONOS conjunctivitis rate should be weighted more than the monotherapy data from SOLO 1. To support this, a study taking a closer look at conjunctivitis in 2629 AD patients in clinical trials found that 8.6%–22.1% of patients had some incidence of conjunctivitis.

Due to these two aspects, we can rely on a significant flow of patients discontinuing the IL4/13 therapies. In our modeling, we take 35% to be the dropout rate, which we base conservatively on the EASI-50 results from SOLO 1. Additionally, real-word data seems to support this, showing 35% of patients showing at least one sign of DUPIXENT non-response.

Benchmarking REZPEG Data Against Competitors

Figures 1 and 2 show summarized endpoints from a fuller analysis of clinical data. JAKis (highlighted in red) lead the pack in efficacy across the board, with EBGLYSS/DUPIXENT following and with NEMLUVIO/Adbry taking the bottom positions; REZPEG is positioned modestly in the middle of the pack. However, it is crucially important to note that it offers best-in-class efficacy, excluding the problematic JAKis and traditional IL4/13 first-line therapies. This puts REZPEG in a position to be the primary benefactor from the non-responder flow we discussed.

Notably, while REZPEG has very competitive EASI and EASI-75 results, there is a larger gap in the more strict EASI-90 and IGA 0/1 (proportion of patients scoring "clear"/"almost clear" on investigators' assessment) endpoints. It may be the case that REZPEG has difficulty achieving full disease clearance.

REZPEG also compares well against therapies in the clinical stage, namely OX40 inhibitors, the next class of drugs to hit the market. From the recent Q2 2025 earnings call, management commented that "reported data of REZPEG at 16 weeks is comparable to 24 weeks of treatment reported with the OX40 agents, but with a faster onset of EASI-75 in its response than what is seen with those agents."

Balance Sheet

Nektar has a strong financial position. They hold no debt, although some pass-through royalty obligations are recorded as debt. The company finished Q2 with a strong cash position of $175.9 million, which amounted to about four quarters of cash burn given the current operating cash flow. Their cash balance was immediately supplemented by an equity offering with gross proceeds of $115 million. Accounting for a 7% in fees, these net proceeds bring up the runway to over six quarters at the current rate. Nektar has guided that it plans to finish the year with $180 million in cash and short-term investments.

However, we model additional equity raises necessary to fund two large Phase 3 registrational studies. We account for this at the bottom of Fig. 8, and the finishing share count is included in our final price target calculations.

Overall, we assess Nektar to maintain a healthy balance sheet, which will be able to protect equity investors reasonably well going forward. With share count not meaningfully increasing over the last several years (aside from the end of the Q2 raise), equity investors can feel relatively secure. However, we note the relatively low insider ownership < 1%.

With strong data justifying a commercially viable position in the market, we can model NKTR using an rNPV methodology.

Model Walkthrough

We start the model with a revenue forecast. With literature showing an annual incidence of 0.56% applied to a US population of 340 million (growing at a rate of 0.45%), we can rely on a slowly growing annual incidence of ~1.9 million patients. With 13% of incident patients treated with systemic/biologic treatments, of which dupilumab makes up 86.5%, we model about 210,000 patients starting on DUPIXENT annually. Applying a 35% non-response rate, we can conservatively estimate an annual incidence of IL4/31 non-responders at 70,000 patients. We split these patients between the three alternative mechanism competitors: 40% of patients take OX-40, 40% of patients take REZPEG, and the remaining 20% are reserved for the worst-in-class (as highlighted by Fig. 1 above), but first-to-market NEMLUVIO. This yields an inflow of 30,000 patients annually started on REZPEG. Additionally, we model an extra 50,000 prescriptions in the launch year, based on NEMLUVIO achieving a run rate of 6,000 monthly prescriptions in the seventh month of launch. With a much better efficacy profile than NEMLUVIO, we do not expect REZPEG to underperform this benchmark. For pricing, we can expect a list price of at least $48,000, although several competitors are priced at a premium, as depicted in Table 1. We also use a CAGR of 7% to match the growth of DUPIXENT's list price since it was originally priced at $37,000 in 2017. We model a gross to net ratio of 50%; such high rebates are typically associated with competitive chronic and common conditions like AD. Finally, we use a medication possession ratio of 80%, which is supported by literature. The resultant revenue schedule can be seen in Fig. 4.

Remaining model assumptions are as follows:

  1. Gross margin is taken at 80%
  2. G&A expenses are increased by 6% 2026-2029, such that they reach levels (~$120 million) seen just prior to the expected bempeg launch in 2022.
  3. Selling and marketing expenses are based off a sales force of 100 people, with a fully loaded cost per specialty sales rep of $228,000 (flexed with inflation at 3%). Marketing expenses are modeled as an additional 3% of revenue.
  4. Interest expenses are continued at $28 million per year, as seen for the last few years. Readers should note that this relates to the amortization of pass-through royalty revenue to which Nektar sold its rights-a purely non-cash accounting transaction.
  5. Interest income is based on the projected cash balance and the 5.68% effective interest rate earned in 2024 off of the company's cash and short-term investments.
  6. Corporate tax rates are assumed to be at 20%.
  7. Research and development expenses are modeled according to a separate schedule outlined in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7.

Research and Development Model

For REZPEG R&D costs, we model two 600 patient Phase 3 studies run from with initial dosing occurring in H1 2026 and completing at the end of H2 2027. Hence, we split the costs of these two trials across 2026 and 2027 equally. We base the cost estimate off an estimate of $41,117, adjusted for inflation to $56,000 per patient. We make sure to include $2.6 million in PDUFA fees for 2027. Furthermore, we model a lower burn rate in 2028 during the registrational phase and no R&D expenditure following launch, for simplicity. We continue to model the same rate of burn for NKTR-255 and NKTR-0165 for simplicity. Personnel and other R&D costs are modeled to grow at 3% with inflation.

With R&D modeling complete, we are able to complete our NPV modeling. Fig. 8 shows pre-launch modeling, while Fig. 9 shows post-launch modeling.

We adjust the net income to unlevered FCF in Fig. 10 and Fig. 11.

Price Targets

Fig. 12 shows that NKTR seems to be within the margin of error fair value at the 20% mark but stands to see significant upside, up to 50%, as we de-risk to 15%. The value is highly sensitive to the discount rate because of the long path to market, with the company only generating revenue in 2029. Decreasing the discount rate by just 5% to 15% presents a fair value of $42.21, up from $28.57.

A Note on Ongoing Litigation

A favorable resolution to an ongoing lawsuit with Eli Lilly could be materially accretive for Nektar Therapeutics. Nektar's claim stems from the terminated 2017 co-development deal with Eli Lilly, where it alleges that Lilly breached the agreement and mishandled REZPEG trial analyses after acquiring a competing asset (which went on to become EBGLYSS). With strong data backing REZPEG's efficacy, this contradicts the analysis that Lilly published and appears to support Nektar's claim to damages. Any cash recovery could extend the runway and moderate dilution risk.

Conclusion

Overall, we consider Nektar to be fairly valued (at our preferred 20% discount rate) without a clear-cut valuation gap at the current price. However, we will continue to watch the company, especially for the series of positive catalysts in late 2025 and early 2026. Of particular importance to us are the following:

  1. A potentially promising readout for AA could be on the books for December 2025, a disease with IL2 at the center of its pathogenesis.
  2. A potentially favorable outcome from that lawsuit against Eli Lilly, now backed by strong efficacy data from REZOLVE-AD to back material damages.
  3. Potential benefits from the approval that Nektar commented that it is "certainly looking at monetizing that asset" in its recent earnings call.

At the same time, we'd like to highlight some potential downsides, as listed below:

  1. A cash balance insufficient to fund Phase 3 trials spells dilution in early/mid 2026. Although we have made our model with this dilution in mind, it may occur at terms less favorable to the current market price and hence result in more market share loss.
  2. AA is a notoriously difficult indication to treat. Despite many attempts, no therapies have been approved outside of the risk-bearing JAK inhibitor class.

data.title: French Growth Stalls As Business Sentiment Falters
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier

Beneath the surface, sectoral divergences are sharpening in France. Retail sentiment plunged by seven points in just one month, dragged down by deteriorating expectations and weak order intentions. In contrast, construction is showing signs of life, with entrepreneurs more upbeat about future activity. Industry remains cautious, with sentiment unchanged, while services are slipping slightly. Taken together, these indicators suggest a relatively flat growth profile for the third quarter of 2025.

Underlying weaknesses

On paper, France’s GDP performance in the first half of this year looks decent given global headwinds, coming in at +0.1% in the first quarter and +0.3% in the second – better than expected. But the headline rate masks a more fragile reality: growth has leaned heavily on inventory accumulation. Stock contributions were striking, with +0.7ppt in the first quarter and +0.5ppt in the second. Strip those out, and the economy would have been contracting.

Private consumption is retreating, investment is soft, and net exports are a drag. The engine is fundamentally stalled, and there’s little to suggest a restart is imminent.

Labour market conditions are also deteriorating. Net job losses have mounted over recent quarters, and in August, the National Institute for Statistics and Economic Studies (INSEE) business survey showed further weakening in employment sentiment. Households remain anxious about unemployment, keeping the savings rate elevated. Investment remains subdued amid uncertainty and high long-term rates.

Fiscal policy is set to tighten, weighing on public spending. Political uncertainty around the budget vote could further erode confidence. Add to that persistent global trade disruptions and a stronger euro, and it’s hard to see exports stepping up as a growth driver anytime soon.

Outlook: Cautious and constrained

We continue to expect only modest growth for the French economy at +0.1% in the third quarter of 2025, +0.6% for the full year, and +0.8% in 2026. A cautious trajectory, mirroring an economy that is moving but without momentum.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Best-In-Class Deserves A Higher Multiple (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A Blowout 4Q FY2025

Palo Alto Networks (PANW) once again proved itself in 4Q FY2025, delivering a blockbuster earnings report with both revenue and non-GAAP EPS ahead of consensus. Most encouragingly, revenue reaccelerated on robust demand for Next Generation Security [NGS] solutions, while operating margin also jumped higher. This combination (revenue YoY growth and non-GAAP operating margin) reached 46.4%, well above the Rule of 40 threshold. FCF more than doubled, with a continued increase in FCF margin, further supporting a premium valuation. Management also issued a strong FY2026 outlook, sending the stock higher in the aftermarket.

In my previous analysis, I upgraded the stock from Sell to Hold, citing that PANW reclaimed the Rule of 40 in 1Q FY2025, after dipping to 39% in 4Q FY2024. At that time, RPO had stabilized near 20% levels after a significant growth slowdown. More recently, PANW has demonstrated a major rebound in RPO to 24% YoY, showing stronger demand. Its revenue YoY growth and non-GAAP operating margin have continued to improve, which is encouraging.

Despite the current lofty equity market backdrop, I believe PANW still has room to move higher, as its EV/Sales TTM is 13x, down from 15x at the time of my last rating. Since then, the stock has declined 6%. Therefore, I upgraded the rating further to Buy from Hold, as all financial metrics are improving with no signs of slowdown.

Rule of 40 Remains Intact

PANW remained well above the Rule of 40 threshold in 4Q FY2025, improving to 46.1% and approaching FY2023 levels. This is a key measure of software company performance, which reflects the balance of growth and profitability. The company has now achieved four consecutive quarters above 40%. The strong growth was supported by $545 million of AI ARR, which grew 250% YoY in 4Q. This AI portion relatively accounts for 10% of NGS ARR, but its contribution is expected to increase meaningfully over the next several years.

The 46.1% result was largely driven by a 290-bps sequential expansion in 4Q FY2025 non-GAAP operating margin, compared with 130 bps sequential increase in 4Q FY2024, highlighting stronger operating efficiency.

While management did not provide non-GAAP operating margin guidance for 1Q FY2026, they projected FY2026 margin of 29.5% at the midpoint of the range. Based on 15% revenue growth for 1Q FY2026, we can calculate PANW is still expected to deliver 44.5% in the coming quarter. This indicates that PANW still can sustain a healthy balance of growth and margin profile, which is a bullish signal for the stock.

Contract Backlog Growth Is Accelerating

PANW's demand remains very strong, supported by accelerating growth in RPO. Remaining Performance Obligations, namely RPO, represent the total contract backlog that will be recognized as revenue over time. This is a key input for calculating booking demand. As shown on the chart, RPO grew 24% YoY in 4Q FY2025, reversing its prior downtrend. For 1Q FY2026, the RPO is expected to grow 23%, indicating resilient demand and high quality of future revenue streams. However, FY2026 guidance of 17%-18% suggests growth may come down after 1Q.

During the 4Q FY2025 earnings call, management emphasized the market's shift toward platformization, which is integrated platforms where multiple solutions are bundled together, rather than sold separately. They noted that this platformization strategy is driving superior financial performance, and its net retention rate among platform customers reached 120% in 4Q FY2025, with nearly 0 churn. PANW expects the number of platformizations to more than double over the next 5 years, signaling that the company is well-positioned to sustain strong growth momentum.

CyberArk Deal Could Drive Further Long-Term Growth

In late July, PANW announced the acquisition of CyberArk (CYBR), a leader in Identity Security and Privileged Access Management, for $25 billion. The deal, expected to close in 2H FY2026, will further strengthen PANW's platformization strategy by enabling customers to consolidate identity security with PANW's existing firewall and broader security portfolio. Furthermore, this industry consolidation also reinforces PANW's leadership in an increasingly competitive cybersecurity industry.

In the earnings call, management also highlighted that the combined offering should drive stronger cross sell opportunities and accelerate adoption of PANW's platforms. According to the company's statement, "The transaction is expected to be immediately accretive to Palo Alto Networks revenue growth and gross margin." Despite a strong 1Q FY2026 outlook, the guidance does not include any impacts from the CyberArk deal.

FCF Jumped 100% in 4Q FY2025

PANW's FCF jumped 100% YoY in 4Q, with its FCF margin expanding to 37.6%, up from 21.3% in 4Q FY2024. This showed a strong rebound from a weaker 1H FY2025. On an annual basis, we can see that its FCF margin has been stable at +38% over the past three years, despite seasonal impact from deferred payments.

The company guided an adjusted FY2026 FCF margin of 38% to 39%, in-line with the current trend. Over the long term, PANW expects its FCF margin to increase to 40% in FY2028, driven by M&A related synergies from CyberArk integration. This should help valuation multiples, such as P/E, come down faster as earnings grow higher.

Is the Stock Expensive?

Yes, PANW is currently trading at a premium valuation, with a non-GAAP P/E fwd of 47.7x. However, we notice the valuation across leading cybersecurity stocks remains elevated, which helps mitigate concerns about PANW's multiple given its strong financial performance. While PANW's P/E has historically stayed at an elevated level, it's currently about 17% below its 5-year average.

On EV/sales fwd, PANW's 11.3x appears relatively reasonable, compared to Zscaler's (ZS) 15.4x, CrowdStrike Holdings' (CRWD) 21x, and CYBR's 16.2x.

Given its Rule of 40 strength, accelerating ARR, improving in RPO growth, expanding margins, and robust FCF generation, I believe PANW deserves to trade at a higher valuation. That said, the stock still has upside potential in the near term, since it hasn't moved that much over the past 12 months, and the recent pullback from the July high could be a good entry point.

Conclusion

In summary, PANW's 4Q FY2025 results and FY2026 guidance point to sustained growth momentum, driven by accelerating demand and meaningful success for its platformization strategy. The recent deal with CyberArk adds accretive synergies, with management targeting a 40% FCF margin by FY2028, signaling steady expansion in the next three years. While the stock trades at a premium, I don't think the current multiple is overly stretched relative to other leading cybersecurity peers. Given its strong fundamentals, PANW deserves to trade at a modest premium than its historical average. I therefore upgraded to the Buy rating on the stock.
