data.title: How Defense And Infrastructure Spending Impacts European Industrial And Logistics
data.description:
By Daniel Tomaselli, Manager, Global Research

A new tailwind for European logistics

European industrial and logistics real estate is one of our highest-conviction investments. With nearly 40% of existing stock already over 17 years old and increasingly unfit for purpose, we anticipate that the demand-supply imbalance for modern logistics assets is set to widen over the coming years. In our Inside Real Estate Mid-Year Outlook, published in July, we outlined the key structural drivers supporting this thesis. On the supply side, tightening sustainability standards and land scarcity are expected to constrain future development, amid the increasing complexity of ground-up projects and the high cost of upgrading older assets to meet regulatory requirements. On the demand side, Europe’s relatively low (and uneven) e-commerce penetration, combined with the growing importance of warehouse automation and sophistication, is expected to support leasing activity, particularly for modern and energy- efficient assets located along the continent’s main logistics corridors and end-customer clusters.

More recently, a powerful new demand catalyst has started to emerge. The current geopolitical climate, marked by the war in Ukraine and the prospect of reduced U.S. engagement abroad, has fundamentally changed European priorities and its fiscal stance. Up to the end of 2024, markets were broadly anticipating a prolonged phase of fiscal consolidation in Europe, aiming at reducing the excess levels of government debt accumulated to support businesses and households coping with the negative effects of the global pandemic first and the energy crisis later. However, during the first months of 2025, the new strategic imperative of strengthening national security has prompted a decisive policy shift, with governments abandoning austerity in favor of increased investment in defense and infrastructure. This new direction was cemented during the last NATO summit in June, when most European members agreed to raise core military spending from the current 2.0% to 3.5% of GDP, plus another 1.5% in related infrastructure, by 2035.

Exhibit 1: European NATO members pledged to gradually increase defense spending to 3.5%

Additional capital required from current levels to reach the 3.5% annual target, $bn

This vast amount of capital is expected to be a significant catalyst for the European economy, and we believe it represents an additional tailwind for the industrial and logistics sector. Our conviction is validated by the following arguments:

  • Public investments can revive the European economy, whose performance has been sluggish over the last four years. Indeed, economists now expect the Eurozone’s growth to accelerate in 2026 and thereafter, buoyed by fiscal stimulus linked to defense and infrastructure projects and more accommodative monetary policy. Forecasts project the Eurozone economy to reach 1.1% in 2026 and 1.5% in 2027, marking a substantial recovery from the sluggish performance of the last few years. This shift in momentum places Europe in a relatively good position, enhancing the demand drivers for commercial real estate, including the industrial and logistics sector.
  • Defense spending does not only concern bullets and artillery, but also modern technologies such as automation, AI, cybersecurity, satellite systems, sensors and robotics. Advancements in these fields could foster innovations and produce spillover effects into other industries, generating broader productivity gains and potentially contribute to narrow the technological gap between Europe and the U.S. – labor productivity in Europe increased by 29% since 1990, compared to 70% in America over the same period, according to The Conference Board(1). Notable examples of military technologies that have found widespread civilian and commercial use in the past include the GPS, internet, advanced materials, drones, jet engines, microwave technology, nuclear energy, and radar systems.
  • Investment in defense could help repurpose spare manufacturing capacity and absorb skilled workers from declining sectors, such as automotive (European automotive production was down 23% in 2024 compared to its peak in 2017). For example, last month The Economist alongside other major news outlets reported that some of the largest European defense and aerospace groups—including Rheinmetall, Hensoldt, Renk, Airbus, Leonardo, Thales and KNDS—are all negotiating to take over factories from stressed firms in other industries, as well as offering to hire workers they are about to let go, to ramp up capacity. EY, a consultancy firm, estimated that higher defense spending in Europe could create 500,000 jobs, of which 150,000 in the actual defense sector and the remaining in associated supply chains.
  • Defense investments are likely to increase the demand for industrial and logistics real estate assets, particularly in and around those key clusters where the production of military and aerospace equipment takes place, or near airbases, defense research centers and ports. In fact, as orders increase, manufacturers will require additional space for component production, assembly, storage and distribution of these systems. At the same time, “overseas groups will be looking to set up local production facilities to ensure they are not locked out of Europe’s spending spree”, according to the Financial Times.

Exhibit 2: Fiscal spending in Europe expected to drive stronger growth

Germany and Euro area GDP growth forecast, percentage

The benefits of these investments will be felt broadly across Europe. Many regions and logistics hubs—including in the UK, Italy, and the Netherlands—stand to benefit from a new wave of fiscal stimulus for defense and infrastructure. However, we expect Germany and France to be the countries with the biggest upside potential. Thus, in the following sections, we focus our analysis on these two markets as a reflection of a broader trend shaping the European industrial and logistics landscape. Insights on other key markets and metro areas are available on request.

The end of German austerity supports a renewed positive outlook for industrial and logistics

The German industrial and logistics real estate sector endured a prolonged period of cyclical headwinds. The German economy was the most exposed to the energy crises that followed the Russian invasion of Ukraine, due to its geographical proximity, lack of alternative energy sources, and a heavy exposure to the manufacturing sector, including energy intensive heavy industries—such as chemicals, metals production, and building materials—whose competitiveness relied upon the availability of cheap fossil fuel imports from Russia. Meanwhile, the automotive sector, a pillar of the German economy accounting for 5% of its GDP and roughly 15%-20% of its exports, has also been under strain amid growing global competition, particularly from Chinese EV brands, and declining new car registrations. Thus, when the ECB started to restrict its monetary policy, both investor sentiment and occupier demand for industrial space weakened.

Transaction activity showed early signs of recovery in 2024, even though volumes remain below the long- term average. Preliminary figures for H1 2025 indicate a further improvement compared to the same period last year, although investors continued to defer decisions amid ongoing trade and geopolitical volatility. Yields for prime logistics assets held firm at 4.3% for the third consecutive quarter after compressing by 10bps from peak, reflecting a stabilizing investment environment and improving investor confidence.

The occupier market has moderated over the past few years, but conditions are showing signs of improvement. Demand across key cities totaled 2.1 million sq. m in 2024—below the five-year average—according to data from Colliers. Some of this softness reflects more challenging operating conditions, such as intensifying competition facing Germany’s automotive manufacturing sector. However, demand has also been constrained by a limited supply of available stock in certain markets, including Hamburg, Munich, and Frankfurt. Encouragingly, demand has begun to accelerate in the first half of 2025, driven largely by logistics providers targeting smaller units (1,000–3,000 sq. m). Rental growth has remained well above the rate of general inflation, even as it continues to normalize from the pandemic-driven surge in 2022. Recent data from MSCI indicates that annual rental growth has slowed to just under 3% nationally, broadly in line with its long-term trend.

Exhibit 3: German logistics rental growth outpaced inflation over the last three years

Industrial/logistics annual rental growth and CPI, percentage change

Prime and average absolute rents for selected German logistics market, €/sq. meter/month

Source: MSCI, Colliers, Principal Real Estate, Q2 2025

Why should investors consider German industrial and logistics? The reason is an unprecedented shift in fiscal policy.

Germany is a country well known for its fiscal austerity and debt aversion. Back in 2009, in the aftermath of the financial crisis, the government coalition led by Angela Merkel passed a constitutional provision limiting annual structural deficits to 0.35% of GDP, also referred to as the ‘debt brake’. The measure remained in place for the following 15 years and allowed the country to reduce its debt-to-GDP ratio by 18 percentage points, from 81% in 2010 to 63% in 2025. However, recent events, including the Russian invasion of Ukraine, a sluggish economy, and the ambivalent behavior of the U.S. towards the NATO alliance, have all led to an unexpected change. After years of self-imposed austerity, in March 2025, the German parliament passed a historic package of constitutional reforms aiming at unleashing hundreds of billions of euros in new borrowing to bolster the country’s ageing infrastructure and defense capacity. The package includes three key measures: it exempts defense spending from the country’s constitutional debt break limits; it established a €500 billion infrastructure investment vehicle of the duration of twelve years (of which €100 billion for climate action and energy transition); and it introduces some debt flexibility for the sixteen regional governments which from now on one can borrow rather than running a strict balanced budget. As a whole, these reforms can unleash up to one trillion euros of capital over the next twelve years, according to Bloomberg estimates. This is a significant amount that is expected to boost German economic growth from 0.2% in 2025 to 1.1% and 1.6% in 2026 and 2027, respectively, as indicated by the latest Bloomberg economists survey conducted in early July.

The country’s defense industry clusters will be the primary beneficiary of this shift.

Notable ones are Bremen, Bremerhaven, Cologne, Dusseldorf, Hamburg, Kassel, Kiel, Munich and Stuttgart. Some of these locations align with Germany’s main logistics real estate markets. Take Munich as an example. The city has a logistics catchment population of 32 million within a 4.5-hour drive time. It acts as a good base for occupiers serving the southern German market or as a regional hub to store goods coming from southern and eastern Europe. Historically, Munich hosts the headquarters and production facilities of well-established defense and aerospace companies, such as MTU Aero Engines, Hensoldt, Airbus, and KNDS. More recently, the city has emerged as one of the main European centers for defense tech, receiving almost one billion dollars of venture capital funding in 2024, according to Advant, a law firm. As most of these companies have already announced programs to expand their workforce and invest several million in capital expenditure projects to cater to growing demand, it is reasonable to assume real estate assets in

the region will offer superior returns over the next few years. This is particularly true when considering that the current stock of modern logistics in Munich is relatively small—estimated at two million square meters—and vacancy rates are already exceptionally tight, hovering at 1.5% as of the start of 2025, according to Colliers.

Exhibit 4: Germany’s defense spending is expected to increase by 70% over the next four years

Source: Bloomberg, Principal Real Estate, June 2025

France’s prominence in the global defense and aerospace sectors sets the stage for industrial and logistics upside

In France, the industrial and logistics real estate market experienced a clear moderation over the last three years, following a record demand in 2021. The market was initially buoyed by a surge in e-commerce and supply chain restructuring during the pandemic, which drove high levels of take-up and development. However, as the pandemic-driven momentum naturally subsided, logistics take-up in the key city markets declined for three consecutive years to more normalized levels, down from 2.4 million sq. meters in 2021 to around 1.3 million sq. meters in 2024, according to PMA. The moderation was mostly driven by the larger format as occupiers grew more selective in response to changing market conditions and a worsening operating environment, including rising borrowing costs and weakening consumer confidence. Fortunately, France was less exposed to the energy price shock that followed the Russian invasion of Ukraine, due to its exposure to nuclear power—between 70% and 80% of the country’s electricity supply is generated from nuclear sources. This favorable energy mix reduced France’s exposure to volatile gas prices, helping to keep inflation at more manageable levels. While inflation exceeded 11% in Germany, Italy, and the UK, it peaked at just 7.3% in France, placing less pressure on household disposable income and companies’ profit margins.

Rising operational costs, alongside tightening sustainability regulations, have contributed to a shift in occupier demand toward energy-efficient facilities with high specifications and ESG credentials. Modern logistics facilities have consistently accounted for the majority of take-up over the past 24 months. In contrast, the vacancy rate for older assets has increased, pushing the overall sector average above 6% in Q2 2025, according to CBRE. Meanwhile, annual rental growth peaked at 7.7% in Q3 2023 and gradually softened to 2.3% in Q1 2025, in line with the long-term average, according to MSCI. However, there is a significant disparity in performance between good-quality modern assets in the right location compared to outdated facilities.

Capital markets activity has declined, as investor sentiment in France was affected by elevated interest rates and a prolonged period of political uncertainty that followed inconclusive parliamentary elections. However, while transaction volumes in the office and retail sectors have remained muted—2024 marked the second-lowest annual volume for French offices since records began in 2007—the industrial and logistics sector rebounded by 20% last year. This recovery was driven by growing demand for modern assets in key logistics hubs such as Paris, Lyon, and Marseille. Meanwhile, prime logistics yields compressed by 10 basis points to 4.9% as of Q1 2025, after peaking at 5.0% in 2024.

Exhibit 5: Take-up declined, but vacancies remain close to historic low levels

Prime logistics take-up, million sq. meters

Prime logistics vacancy rate, percentage

Source: PMA, Principal Real Estate, Q4 2024

Going forward, we believe increasing government spending for defense projects is set to sustain industrial and logistics real estate demand further. France has less fiscal room compared to Germany due to a much higher debt-to-GDP ratio of 113% (behind only Greece and Italy) and a budget deficit of 5.8% of GDP (far higher than the EU limit of 3 percent). Finding the capital required to incrementally rise core military spending from the current 2.1% of GDP to 3.5% by 2035 may prove challenging. However, even assuming very conservative projections, it is reasonable to believe that French firms will benefit equally from soaring demand overseas. This is because France is a major global player in the defense and aerospace industries, with a very strong orientation towards exports, which account for 82% of the industry’s consolidated turnover. For example, France is the world’s largest exporter of aircrafts and spacecrafts, and the world’s second largest exporter of arms, after the United States. Indeed, France’s industrial base comprises several leading international companies, such as Airbus, Dassault Aviation and Thales, supported by roughly 5,000 small-medium enterprises. Overall, these firms provide a full spectrum of military production capabilities, ranging from small tactical weapons to nuclear-powered aircraft carriers and other advanced systems that are difficult to source elsewhere in Europe. Thus, the effects of growing domestic and international demand should produce a significant positive impact. French companies are already racing to hire new staff and ramp up production.

The French defense industry is geographically widespread, reflecting a longstanding government policy of distributing military bases and manufacturing facilities across the country. This approach supports regional economies, bolsters underdeveloped areas, and enhances national security. Our geospatial analysis of the twenty largest publicly listed European defense companies reveals that, in France, these firms operate 211 sites located in 100 geographic zones, each measuring 250 sq. km, as shown in Exhibit 6 on the next page. Some of these facilities are located in or near France’s major logistics real estate markets, including Paris, Lyon, Marseille and Lille, the Le Havre/Rouen and Bordeaux. Lyon, for instance, is the second-largest logistics hub in terms of modern stock, with approximately 5.5 million square meters. Strategically positioned along the country’s main trade corridor connecting Paris to Marseille, it serves as a key distribution center, reaching a population of 22 million people within a 4.5-hour drive. The city also sits at the heart of the Auvergne-Rhône- Alpes region, a major aerospace and defense cluster, anchored by major manufacturers such as Safran, Dassault Aviation, and KNDS.

France’s vacancy rate, currently hovering around 4.9%, remains low compared to the historical average of approximately 8%. This reflects a structurally tight market, supported by organic demand from established logistics operators, constrained new development, and limited land availability. Looking ahead, as European governments begin to increase defense budgets from 2026 onwards, France’s prominent position in the global aerospace and military sectors is expected to provide additional support for both occupancy and income growth.

Exhibit 6: France is the World’s second-largest aerospace exporter

Top 5 aerospace exporters, $bn, 2024

France’s defense industry map(3)

Source: Bloomberg, World’s Top Exports, Principal Real Estate, June 2025

Supply slowdown reinforces the positive outlook for European logistics

Beyond the specific examples of Germany and France, the broader European industrial and logistics sector continues to demonstrate strong fundamentals and diminishing supply risk. Indeed, development activity has moderated significantly across most European countries. Total logistics completions fell by approximately 20% in 2024, the first annual decline in over a decade. The correction reflects a combination of factors, including a period of elevated financing costs and increasingly stringent construction regulations. The UK, Spain, the Netherlands, and Germany recorded the sharpest drops in completions, as shown in the exhibit below. Development starts, a leading indicator for future completions, suggest that project deliveries could decline by a further 11% in 2025. The slowdown is expected to affect all warehouse size bands, with a more pronounced impact in core logistics hubs where land availability is increasingly constrained.

Exhibit 7: Completions declined across most European countries

Logistics completions by country, million sqm

Moderating supply will support rental growth and occupancy levels. Over the last two years, vacancy rates have trended upwards in several European markets as operators' expansion programs normalized after the pandemic boom, consumer confidence was eroded by the energy crisis, and speculative deliveries outpaced demand in some regions. However, despite all the headwinds that the sector has endured, the European logistics vacancy rate stood at the healthy level of 4.6% by the end of 2024, which is well below the long-term average, reflecting robust fundamentals. Against this backdrop, and with demand gaining renewed momentum, also thanks to the fiscal stimulus for defense and infrastructure projects described in this paper, we see a favorable supply-demand dynamic emerging. These conditions support our view that the logistics sector is well-positioned to outperform the broader commercial real estate market in the new cycle.

Exhibit 8: Vacancy rates remain near historical lows in most core markets

Logistics vacancy rates, percentage

Conclusion

The European industrial and logistics real estate market has found a new equilibrium, following a rapid expansion during the pandemic and a prolonged period of macroeconomic uncertainty. The occupier market has now stabilized and is positioned for further improvement, underpinned by structural drivers such as e-commerce growth, automation, tightening ESG regulations, and a slowdown in new supply. Additionally, the mobilization of capital towards defense and infrastructure projects is set to provide a powerful new catalyst for demand, particularly in and around key logistics hubs and defense-aerospace clusters, as illustrated in the cases of Germany and France. Meanwhile, capital values have already rebounded by 4% as of Q1 2025, after a peak-to-trough correction of 21%, indicating the market has entered the early stages of a new cycle. With investor sentiment improving and debt once again accretive, conditions are in place for the recovery to gain momentum. Thus, we believe, the second half of the year offers a compelling entry point for investors seeking to secure durable income and long-term capital appreciation in a sector with robust fundamentals and solid growth prospects.

Footnotes

(1) The Conference Board is a non-profit business think-tank that provides insights on the global economy, business performance, labor markets, and consumer behavior.

(2) Based on 147 German assets of the 20 defense companies: Airbus SE, Babcock, BAE Systems PLC, Dassault Aviation SA, Fincantieri SpA, Hensoldt AG, Indra Sistemas SA, KNDS NV, Kongsberg Gruppen ASA, Leonardo SpA, Melrose Industries PLC, MTU Aero Engines AG, Renk AG, Rheinmetall AG, Rolls-Royce Holdings PLC, Saab AB, Safran SA, SES SA, Thales SA.

(3) Based on the French 211 assets of top 20 defense companies: Airbus SE, Babcock, BAE Systems PLC, Dassault Aviation SA, Fincantieri SPA, Hensoldt AG, Indra Sistemas SA, KNDS NV, Kongsberg Gruppen ASA, Leonardo SPA, Melrose Industries PLC, MTU Aero Engines AG, Renk AG, Rheinmetall AG, Rolls-Royce Holdings PLC, Saab AB, Safran SA, SES SA, Thales SA.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: United Natural Foods: Cash Flow Improving, Margins Inching Up
data.description:
I'm neutral on United Natural Foods (UNFI). The business is stabilizing, and execution has improved this year. Q3 FY25 showed better EBITDA, solid free cash flow, and continued deleveraging. Management also updated full-year guidance after the June cyber incident and still expects meaningfully positive FCF. But this is a low-margin, scale game with customer concentration and ongoing network changes. I see progress, not proof. I rate the stock a HOLD while I watch for sustained margin gains and lower leverage.

What UNFI is and why its structure matters

UNFI is a leading grocery wholesaler across the U.S. and Canada, serving 30,000+ customer locations with ~250,000 SKUs from 52 distribution centers spanning ~30 million sq. ft. That footprint gives UNFI national reach and scale, but it also cements the company in a business where margins are thin and operating discipline matters every day.

Customer concentration is a standing risk, and a key relationship was renewed. UNFI amended and restated its distribution agreement with its largest customer, extending the relationship through May 20, 2032. This lowers medium-term headline risk, but it doesn't change the fact that big customers hold bargaining power in a low-margin model.

The recent print: better EBITDA and cash generation

Q3 FY25 results were cleaner. Net sales were $8.059B, adjusted EBITDA $157M, and adjusted EPS $0.44. Free cash flow in the quarter was $119M. These are the kinds of numbers I want to see if the turnaround is real: more cash, improving operations, and no new balance sheet surprises.

Leverage is moving the right way. As of Q3 FY25, UNFI showed net debt/adjusted EBITDA of ~3.3x, down from higher levels through FY24, with over $1.4B of available liquidity during the period. The plan is to reach around 2.5x by year-end FY26 if execution holds.

Strategy and network optimization

UNFI is simplifying the network to drive efficiency and shrink costs. In FY25, the company consolidated two DCs, closed a third in the Central region, and later agreed with a Northeast customer to end a supply agreement, leading to a planned Allentown, PA, DC closure in early FY26.

That customer represented ~$1B of annual sales, which sounds scary, but management frames the move as part of a longer-term push to improve efficiency and mix. Meanwhile, a new 1.3M sq. ft. DC in Manchester, PA, is live (with full-case automation added in Q3), and a ~1.0M sq. ft. DC in Sarasota, FL, is slated to open in the first half of FY26.

This is the right direction. It reduces complexity, improves throughput, and supports service levels for the largest national customers. But network work is never "done," and there are execution risks each time volume moves.

The cyber incident and the updated FY25 outlook

In June, UNFI suffered an unauthorized incident on its technology systems. On July 16, 2025, management laid out the impact and updated FY25 guidance.

The company estimated lost sales of ~$350-$400M and an adjusted EBITDA impact of ~$40-$50M, with a revised FY25 outlook to net sales of $31.6-$31.8B, adjusted EBITDA of $535-$565M, adjusted EPS of $0.40-$0.80, and free cash flow = $200M. Given that context, Q3's on-plan execution was a positive surprise.

I'm not going to overreact to a one-off disruption. What matters is whether UNFI can keep delivering on the margin and cash targets it set before the incident. The updated outlook suggests management still expects to do so.

Profitability: the ceiling is the debate

UNFI's biggest challenge is structural. Grocery distribution is a low-margin, high-volume business. The company calls this out itself. Margins are sensitive to inflation/deflation, supplier promotions, labour, fuel, and the ongoing consolidation of retail customers. That's the core reason I'm not chasing a rerating yet. Execution can improve outcomes, but the economic "ceiling" is set by the model.

That said, management is pushing the right levers: network optimization, automation, shrink control, and leaner ops. Q3's 21% y/y adjusted EBITDA growth points to early traction. I want to see two more quarters that look like this before I move off HOLD.

Balance sheet and cash flow

The debt stack is manageable with the Term Loan B due 2031 and 6.75% senior notes due 2028, backed by ample revolver capacity. Liquidity is healthy, and the company has been chipping away at net debt as FCF improves. The target to ~2.5x leverage by FY26 is reasonable if EBITDA meets the updated plan and working capital stays tidy.

Valuation vs. peers

Net-net, UNFI looks optically cheap on throughput metrics (P/S, EV/S) and on PEG, but still pricey on near-term earnings. The gap narrows by FY3 as estimates rise. On EV/EBITDA, it sits between higher-margin distributors and lower-multiple peers. For a full re-rating toward the leaders, I still want to see sustained margin expansion and continued deleveraging.

Seeking Alpha Quant Rating Breakdown

The Quant system currently paints a generally positive picture of UNFI, with some important nuances.

Valuation: A+

It's no surprise that the quant model awards UNFI its top grade on valuation. By multiple comparisons, the stock looks very cheap. For instance, metrics like EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales, P/Cash Flow, and price-to-book are well below sector averages .

The rock-bottom P/S ratio and low EV/EBITDA contribute heavily here. This A+ suggests an attractive value in statistical terms. I agree that UNFI is quantitatively cheap on many fronts, though as discussed, some of that is due to structurally low margins (which the quant model doesn't fully account for qualitatively).

Growth: A+

The mix under the hood is lopsided, which matters for expectations. On the top line, revenue growth (YoY) is 6.62% versus a 2.11% sector median, but forward revenue growth is 1.94% , a notch below the sector's median.

Profit metrics show the same split: EBITDA growth (YoY) and EBIT growth (YoY) are better than the sector, yet forward EBITDA and forward EBIT lag the sector.

EPS is mixed: diluted EPS growth (YoY) is not meaningful off last year's loss, forward diluted EPS lags the sector, but GAAP EPS (fwd) and the 3-5 year EPS CAGR outpace the sector.

Cash flow tells the same story of rebound then normalization: levered FCF growth (YoY) and operating cash flow (YoY) are outpacing the sector, while forward FCF/share and forward operating cash flow are lagging the sector. Working capital and capex are contracting, consistent with a tighter, cash-focused playbook.

Net-net, the Quant Growth grade is being driven by massive year-over-year recovery and a strong long-term EPS CAGR, while near-term forward sub-metrics are softer as comps get tougher and the business normalizes after a big rebound. I read that as "momentum is real, but not linear."

Profitability: D+

Margins sit well below the sector: gross margin 13.36% vs. 35.98% median, EBIT margin 0.64% vs. 9.60%, EBITDA margin 1.67% vs. 13.55%, and net margin -0.21% vs. 4.30%.

Returns tell the same story: ROE -4.11%, ROA -0.89%, and return on total capital 2.45% versus 11.08%, 4.39%, and 7.10% for the sector. Levered FCF margin is 1.04% versus 5.55%, though it's above UNFI's 5-year average (0.82%), which is a small step in the right direction.

The bright spot is asset turnover at 4.24x (A+), far ahead of the 0.86x sector median, consistent with a high-throughput model. Cash from operations is $509M (C+), up vs. the company's 5-year average ($452M). Capex/Sales is 0.88%, well below the 3.17% sector median, which supports cash but also reflects a low-margin, efficiency-driven business.

Bottom line: profitability is still the weak link and leaves little cushion; sustained margin expansion and deleveraging need to do the heavy lifting before I change the rating.

Momentum: A

UNFI's Momentum grade is A, but the path is uneven. Over 1 year, the stock is up 97.8% versus a -5.3% sector median, and the 9-month return is +22.5% versus -1.0%.

Near term, performance cooled: -11.9% over 3 months and -16.3% over 6 months, consistent with the post-Q3 pullback and cyber-incident overhang.

Revisions: D-

Revisions have turned mixed-to-negative near term. Over the last three months for the upcoming fiscal year, EPS saw 0 up revisions and 10 down revisions, while revenue had 9 up and 1 down. Current consensus steps up over time (FY25: $0.64; FY26: $1.40; FY27: $1.86; FY28: $2.21; FY29: $1.37), but the EPS trend line shows a sharp cut in 2023 and only a gradual recovery since, with recent softness.

Key risks

Big customers can reset terms or insource volume, which would hurt throughput and margins. Technology risk isn't theoretical anymore after the June incident; UNFI calls out systems reliability and business continuity as material risk factors. Macro still matters as shoppers trade down, which can alter mix and pricing power. Execution on the network plan and the Allentown exit has to be tight to avoid service issues or unexpected costs.

Bottom line

Q3 shows the plan is working: better EBITDA, better cash, lower leverage. The updated FY25 outlook post-incident still calls for solid FCF. But the business remains low-margin, concentrated, and mid-restructuring. I'm not paying for a full multiple re-rate yet. I stay at HOLD and look for three things to upgrade: (1) two to three more quarters of sustained margin and EBITDA improvement, (2) leverage tracking toward ~2.5x with clean working capital, and (3) evidence that network moves drive lasting cost per case benefits without service slips.

data.title: ECCF: Attractive Term Preferred Equity From ECC, 8% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

We have never covered the Series F Term Preferred Stock Due 2029 (NYSE:ECCF) on the Seeking Alpha platform, yet we own these securities. With the latest Eagle Point Credit (ECC) results out and better than expected NAV performance, we want to revisit the CEF and its term preferred securities in today's macro context.

Some fund analytics first

  • AUM: $1 billion
  • Distribution: 25%
  • Discount to NAV: -8%
  • Leverage: 29%

The fund has now hit $1 billion in assets and represents a major player in the CLO equity space along with Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC).

Q2 2025 Results

After a rocky Q1, the CEF saw a very robust NAV performance in the second quarter, with the net asset value per common share of $7.31 on June 30, 2025, rising from $7.23 in Q1. The management team had the following explanation for the robust performance:

We took advantage of the market dislocation in the second quarter to acquire discounted CLO equity investments that will enhance the company's returns over the long-term," said CEO Thomas P. Majewski. "Despite the dislocation, recurring cash flows from our portfolio remained robust during the quarter, comfortably exceeding our distributions and operating expenses."

A solid NAV performance is very rare because CLO equity by design is set to experience lower NAV as time passes by. As the name implies, CLO equity is 'equity', thus has no principal balance maturity date. The securities trade on an IRR basis, and later in their life, especially when recessions hit, they can completely shut off cash flows until the debt in the capital structure is repaid.

The fund's quarterly performance shows the ability of the manager to pick good credits:

We can see the CEF generating excess cash versus its common share distributions and company expenses.

Investors looking at the CEF and its debt should also look at the fund's total returns in the past few years:

Despite its recent hick-ups in terms of price moves, the CEF has been very good at providing true equity returns in the past five years. A total return analysis shows the fund surpassing the S&P 500 and, more importantly, doing it consistently during various timeframes. Yes, recessions will see ECC's price go down, but the fund is not a money-burning machine like other CEFs but a true total return generator over long periods of time. It comes down to good portfolio management for a very risky but rewarding asset class.

ECCF is Debt, but a Strong NAV Matters

The securities are categorized as 'Term preferred', but they represent debt when it comes to IFRS accounting of the balance sheet. They are also protected by asset coverage ratio covenants, which ensure the CEF will de-leverage if the underlying holdings fall down in value significantly.

Even with the above protections, an investor, however, needs to pay attention to NAV performance and asset manager performance. A strong asset manager will be able to better navigate tough markets, and without a doubt the fund will hit a recessionary environment at some point. The question is not 'if', but 'when'. With its agility, the portfolio manager has shown it has the ability to dynamically adjust when market conditions change and take advantage of new opportunities. The manager's performance also allows the fund to tap holders for new equity, as it did in Q1 2025 when it placed 7.6 million new shares.

The full suite of notes and term preferred shares from the CEF is as follows:

Please note that ECCF is on the shorter maturity side when compared to other outstanding instruments from the fund.

Yield Considerations and Drawdowns

Let us look now at how the securities did in 2025, especially during the April 2025 market risk-off event:

From its historical performance, we can see a very well capped down move here, with drawdowns going to -2.5% on a monthly reading basis. While a low drawdown profile is expected given the debt status, the term preferred shares are indeed very robust. Risk analytics show a 5% volatility figure, which is quite attractive given the high yield. And speaking about yield:

  • Current yield: 7.95%
  • Current price: $25.18
  • Original coupon: 8%

The securities are trading very close to par (0.7% premium) and have a current yield that is pretty much on top of the yield to maturity. The notes mature in January 2029, although they do become redeemable in January 2026. We do not think the CEF will call the notes though, and investors should price their risk and returns to the full maturity, in our opinion.

Macro Picture is Concerning

While nobody can time the market, the current macro picture is indeed concerning. We have very, very tight spreads in both the investment grade and HY world, which means the next move will have to be up. Suffice to look at BBB bond spreads to understand the context:

As reflected by the ICE BofA BBB US Corporate Index OAS, BBB spreads are at the tightest level since 1998. That is not a typo. Can they continue to stay low? Yes. Is this a massive red flag for the next 12 months? You bet. History tells us the next move in spreads will be up, and it will be a very violent move.

We are not claiming that we can time the market, but when conditions are very tight in credit, we would stay away from CLO equity and rather invest in debt secured by such investments. And ECCF is a perfect instrument for that from both a risk and yield perspective.

Conclusion

ECCF is a term preferred security from ECC. Trading lose to par and yielding roughly 8%, the securities represent an attractive proposal in an otherwise overbought market. ECC has done very well in Q2 2025, preserving its NAV and showing the trading acumen of its portfolio managers. We are very hesitant to jump into CLO equity here, though, given the macro conditions, and prefer ECCF instead.

data.title: Coca-Cola: Top Choice In Times Of Uncertainty At An Opportunity Cost
data.description:
Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO) is one of, if not the most, well-known brands in the world, as it is estimated that 94% of the world's population recognises the logo, with an estimated 3% of beverages drunk every day owned by Coca-Cola. Coca-Cola has a huge moat among its competitors, as Coca-Cola is widely available around the world. Coca-Cola has a ~0.44 5Y monthly beta, as volatility is low, evident from its rise in January during uncertainty and being stable since. Coca-Cola's moat is unlikely to be affected anytime soon, as Coca-Cola is one of the top plays for stable dividends and value for investors. Coca-Cola is the perfect stock for traders wanting a stable stock during tough times, but do not expect Coca-Cola to have any exceptional growth rates like big AI anytime soon. Coca-Cola shareholders are also long-term holders, but major indexes have significantly higher returns than Coca-Cola since 2020 as the gap widens. This creates whether holding Coca-Cola is still the long-term play, as opportunity cost is high. Hence, I'm rating Coca-Cola at a 'Hold'.

Thesis

Coca-Cola is one of the most well-known and valuable brands in the world. According to Forbes, Coca-Cola is the 6th most valuable brand in the world. The only 5 brands that Coca-Cola is behind are the biggest tech firms in the world, which shows the extent of Coca-Cola's moat. With Coca-Cola continuing to be one of the most drunk drinks in the world, the moat isn't fading anytime soon. A defensive stock in one's portfolio, Coca-Cola is no doubt one of the top picks.

The S&P 500 (SPY) is achieving all-time highs. There are many arguments for a bubble forming, but the AI boom is very real, as the recent wave of earnings proves this, with Google even increasing capex by $10B during times of uncertainty. Valuations for the SPY keep increasing as tech has been the main driver for the SPY, as the high valuations could create a bubble despite the AI boom being real. As the valuations go up, fears of a bubble forming increase, as the Magnificent 7 consist of Nvidia, Meta, Alphabet, Tesla, Amazon, Apple, and Microsoft now make up 34% of the S&P 500. Since Coca-Cola's moat is wide, one of the least shorted stocks in the SPY, and stable in times of uncertainty, Coca-Cola is always the stock for investors to hold when the market fears.

Despite Coca-Cola's moat, the issue for Coca-Cola is a very weak growth rate. Despite being one of the top defensive stocks and having a higher valuation, the room for Coca-Cola to grow is quite low. So in times of uncertainty, Coca-Cola is set to outperform the market, such as still having a higher YTD return than SPY. Despite the huge rebound since April following the huge dip from January-March, Coca-Cola still outperforms the SPY as times remain uncertain. However, looking at a larger time scale, Coca-Cola underperforms most indexes over a larger time scale. Coca-Cola shareholders tend to hold Coca-Cola over a long period, but the question has to be raised whether it is still the smart play with most indexes' past performance. With limited growth prospects, holding Coca-Cola over the long term may no longer be the play.

Coca-Cola's Moat

Coca-Cola has one of the largest intangible assets a firm can have. Coca-Cola has one of the most well-known brands in the world, as well as its special Coca-Cola recipe. Out of every report that has been published with the most valuable/known brands from different sources like Forbes, Statista, Interbrand, and IG, most reports all show that Coca-Cola is at least in the top 10 when it comes to brand awareness and value. Coca-Cola is sold everywhere in the world except for Cuba (US companies can't trade with Cuba) and North Korea.

This is the strongest part of Coca-Cola's moat, as its product's demand is not heavily affected by macroeconomic headwinds, evident in the downturn earlier in the year. This leads to stable growth in revenue, as investors buy Coca-Cola knowing that it is not volatile and ensures stability, especially in times of uncertainty. On August 19, highly volatile names such as AMD, Palantir, Sofi, Robinhood, and many more all tanked by a minimum of -5%. As investors know that Coca-Cola won't be negatively affected as uncertainty in the market increases, awaiting Jerome Powell's speech on the following day, Coca-Cola gained 1.45% as the S&P 500 went red. Coca-Cola isn't going anywhere, as it is one of the most valuable brands in the world and will likely remain one of the most well-known brands in the world for a few more decades.

Coca-Cola, in its relative sector, is also one of the most efficient firms, as it has economies of scale from producing at a larger scale. Comparing margins to Coca-Cola's competitors, it boasts a higher gross margin than the rest of its peers, as it produces at a larger scale, which suggests that the cost per unit is lower. The gross margin gap isn't that wide, but the operating margin is where Coca-Cola excels. Coca-Cola has an operating margin of 30.65%, as Pepsi, Celsius, and Monster had 13.5%, 10.4%, and 26.93%, respectively. This shows Coca-Cola's competitive advantage as they are more efficient in its operations, as this shows that Coca-Cola has a moat over its competitors and likely won't be affected in the near term. This is evident from their gross margins being higher than their competitors over such a large time scale, dating back years and years whilst being stable.

The only weakness in Coca-Cola's moat is switching costs. As a consumer staple, it doesn't hurt the consumer to try a new beverage off the shelf, as there is no real cost for the consumer except for utility and quality of life. If a consumer wants to have Pepsi today rather than Coca-Cola, there are no limits for the consumer to do that. However, the Coca-Cola formula is loved around the globe and is still the top-selling soft drink in the world. As a business-to-consumer and a business-to-business (focusing on B2C, as B2B leads to selling to consumers anyway), the moat Coca-Cola has is brand loyalty, as its drink is iconic and desirable. Switching costs may be low for consumers, as sales could fall as consumers try new drinks, but evidently this has not happened as Coca-Cola remains the top-selling soft drink after decades of operations. Further brand loyalty is shown as Coca-Cola collaborates with big chains like McDonald's, which exclusively serve Coca-Cola drinks. Brand awareness increases for Coca-Cola, as its legacy is most likely to continue on as it is widely available everywhere and loved by many individuals.

Risks to the Moat

Coca-Cola excels as its brand awareness and visibility are high, as it is able to appeal to most individuals around the globe, as Coca-Cola is always a beverage choice available wherever you are. This is a dual-edged sword of maturity. Since customers already know what Coca-Cola is and how it tastes, unless consumers significantly change their preferences for Coca-Cola, Coca-Cola will struggle with growth. So loyal consumers are able to purchase Coca-Cola anywhere they are, as Coca-Cola can benefit from this, but it comes in exchange for no room for growth as people are aware of Coca-Cola's products already.

This differs from Pepsi with a more diversified portfolio of products, as not essentially 100% of products, which gives Pepsi more potential to grow (but more risky). Despite owning Costa Coffee and other successful acquisitions, these segments are still saturated with competitors like Pret, Starbucks, and Cafe Nero. Despite high loyalty and brand awareness, the moat could be fading as sales could fall due to already being known. With healthy alternatives like Celsius growing at higher rates than Coca-Cola, as forward revenue growth is 30.2% compared to just 3.78% (Seeking Alpha Growth Tab), the moat may be intact and still wide, but local competition is slowly chipping away at the moat with changes in preferences such as healthy initiatives for individuals.

If sales do fall, Coca-Cola's margins could also take a hit, as it will no longer have the economies of scale that it has today. It is not positioned for higher growth as peers like Celsius, as if revenue growth slows and margins contract, it doesn't justify the valuation that Coca-Cola has.

Top Defensive Play, but High Opportunity Cost Raises Concerns

Coca-Cola is a safe play for investors in times of uncertainty, as the share price for Coca-Cola tends to increase during instability and fear increases in the market (start of the year). It has always been a stock that has a low short interest, as short interest is only 0.82%. It is also one of the least shorted S&P 500 stocks in July. With slow, stable growth and a solid dividend yield of ∼3%, it is a top play as a defensive stock in the portfolio. However, is Coca-Cola still worth holding in this new era?

Margins are solid, but growth is slow, as dividend yields continue to attract long-term investors as they know their investment is stable for the long term. My argument is that the compound rate of major indexes performs better than Coca-Cola, so what's the appeal to holding Coca-Cola over major indexes that have proven that they will go up over a long time period? The only counterargument is the time of the market, as if the index during times of uncertainty will be priced at a lower premium, while Coca-Cola will always be priced around the same premium with low volatility. Except for this argument, I don't see why major indexes are a worse hold than Coca-Cola over the long term.

This is the total return of Coca-Cola against the S&P 500.

And this is the price return (no dividends).

We are in the decade of the AI boom, as the AI boom has shown no signs of slowing down anytime soon. Alphabet (GOOGL) in times of uncertainty has increased capex by $10B as earnings support this AI boom. The issue is the valuations and concentration of the technology sector.

From Reuters, this chart showed the rising concentration of the technology sector getting closer and closer to 2000s levels. This implies that returns of the S&P 500 in recent times have been contributed by top AI companies, as top giants like Nvidia, Meta, and Microsoft on a larger scale all outperform the S&P 500. Their increase in concentration is a result of a larger market cap, as their weighting in the index is weighted by their market cap. With their rise of the tech sector market cap as the index's market cap, it suggests that valuations are getting lofty. Coca-Cola will benefit when investors move out of growth stocks from the tech industry and into value stocks but Coca-Cola, but it is a matter of when and if they will.

Growth stocks dominate the market today as investors try to chase the next stock that can multiply itself and multiply fast, such as Nvidia and Palantir, as evident since the tech sector is getting more concentrated in the index. Coca-Cola's against S&P 500 gap is now double, as the gap widened in 2020, as without dividends, the investors who may have missed the dividends significantly miss out, as on a pure stock price return basis, the index outperforms Coca-Cola by 3 times. People hold indexes over a long period of time, so why hold Coca-Cola over major indexes like the S&P 500? The compound rate for Coca-Cola is no longer as impressive, as the price return itself is significantly lower than the S&P 500.

Except for YTD, the S&P 500 dominated Coca-Cola when it comes to returns over a longer period. Coca-Cola ensures stability for the investor, but now it is all about opportunity cost. The index since 2000 has only had 1 instance when the index has consecutive negative years, which was 2000, 2001, and 2002. External shocks like the Global Financial Crisis and COVID-19 only had 1 year of negative returns, followed by consecutive years of positive returns. The opportunity cost for buying Coca-Cola is hence quite high, as the potential returns and solid picks in the market are high.

As the index valuations get higher and higher, as uncertainty strikes the market leading to sell-offs, Coca-Cola in these scenarios will outperform the S&P 500 as it still does YTD after the selloff at the start of the year. However, Coca-Cola isn't really a stock for investors to trade, as it is not volatile but one to hold. Over a long period, Coca-Cola will underperform, as the compound rate isn't as impressive anymore and the growth rate is low. In a bull market, Coca-Cola will underperform the tech sector. Since the April lows, big tech like Nvidia has seen its stock price nearly double. Coca-Cola, on the other hand, is flat. Opportunity cost is high for long-term holders in a bull market, but an opportunity in bear markets. I'm not predicting a bear market, but with high valuations and high concentration, holding Coca-Cola in this scenario isn't that bad to have the portfolio to play defense. Remember, it is all about opportunity cost, as you know what to expect with Coca-Cola stock, but expect volatility with the S&P 500 and technology stocks.

Valuation

Investors are shifting to high-growth stocks, as these stocks are more exciting, as AI stocks trade at significant premiums. For example, Nvidia's current P/E GAAP is ~57 with a sector median of ~28, as top picks trade at premiums to the sector median. The question is whether Coca-Cola deserves a premium. On a P/E non-GAAP, GAAP, and P/S basis, Coca-Cola trades at a premium to its sector. Coca-Cola has a solid wide moat, but with a limited growth rate, the moat is more likely to erode than get wider.

As I have mentioned many times, it is all about opportunity cost when it comes to Coca-Cola: to hold Coca-Cola and be safe or risk and achieve higher returns. I believe there are many better picks than Coca-Cola just based on a valuation and growth basis.

This is Google's valuation metrics. It trades slightly lower on a P/E non-GAAP and GAAP basis, while its P/S levels are at the same of Coca-Cola. Both firms have a ~60% gross margin, but the growth rate of Coca-Cola is 1-4% on a current and forward basis, while Google has a 13%-14% growth rate on a current and forward basis. Google also has a moat in its segment, though it is getting threatened and disrupted; this also goes to Coca-Cola with new competitors like Celsius. The extent of both moats can be argued to determine which is better, but the idea remains that to hold Coca-Cola, the opportunity cost is high. Growth rate remains low, but stability is ensured. It would depend on the investors whether this tradeoff is worth it, as volatility is a huge difference.

Conclusion

Whether Coca-Cola is worth it depends on the investor's risk tolerance, as Coca-Cola is a proven stable stock that has a good dividend yield. In my lower-risk portfolio, I do hold a small position in Coca-Cola, as I like the stability and a stable dividend rate. However, the opportunity cost for Coca-Cola is simply high, as even indexes have outperformed Coca-Cola over the last few years by significant margins, which questions whether Coca-Cola is still worth it to hold long-term. With such a high opportunity cost, I rate Coca-Cola as a Hold with a potential run-up, as growth stocks get dumped is still alive as the S&P 500 hits all-time highs and higher valuations.
